topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the glossy presentation slides spread across his desk, the logos of the acquiring firm gleaming under the fluorescent lights. He traced the outline of the new corporate structure with a trembling finger. "They're selling us out," he muttered, the words tasting like ash in his mouth. He felt a cold, heavy pressure settle in his chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. He looked up, meeting Sarah’s gaze across the cubicle partition. Her expression was carefully neutral, but Elias caught a flicker of something—a shared, unspoken acknowledgment of the precariousness of their position. He pushed his chair back abruptly, the scrape of metal against the carpet sounding unnaturally loud in the quiet office. He needed air. He needed to process the sheer audacity of it all.

[story 2]

"So, this is it then?" Maria asked, her voice dangerously level as she reviewed the termination paperwork. She didn't look up, her focus fixed on the fine print detailing severance packages and non-compete clauses. A tightness began to constrict around her jaw, a muscle she hadn't realized was so tense until it seized control. Every carefully constructed professional facade she wore felt brittle, ready to shatter. She picked up a pen, turning it over and over in her hand until the plastic felt slick against her skin. The air in the conference room suddenly felt thin, suffocating. She wanted to shout, to demand an explanation that would justify the devaluation of years of dedicated work. Instead, she simply placed the pen down with deliberate, almost aggressive softness.

[story 3]

"They think we're just a line item," Ben thought, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge of the mahogany table. The news had hit him like a physical blow, stealing the oxygen from the room. He ran a hand through his already disheveled hair, the gesture jerky and uncontrolled. His breathing became shallow, rapid puffs of air that didn't seem to reach his lungs fully. He looked around the room, at the familiar faces of his colleagues, and saw only a collection of interchangeable components being liquidated. When his manager approached, Ben managed a tight, almost strained smile, the muscles in his face refusing to cooperate with the required pleasantry. His eyes held a deep, simmering resentment, a silent declaration that he would not simply accept this erasure.
